name: iRacing
bestFor: League racing, oval/road discipline, endurance teams, and drivers who care about clean competition.
hardwareThatMatters: A stable cockpit, load-cell pedals, a 9-12 Nm direct-drive base, and triples or a serious ultrawide.
watchTheTrap: The subscription plus cars and tracks can outrun your hardware budget if you impulse-buy series.

name: Assetto Corsa EVO
signal: The next big PC sim-racing watchlist title.
bestFor: Drivers who want modern physics, broad car culture, mod-adjacent energy, and a rig that can grow with the platform.
hardwareThatMatters: A direct-drive base with clean detail, load-cell pedals, and a display setup with enough FOV to read traffic.
watchTheTrap: Early-platform excitement can move faster than hardware support and settings wisdom. Buy a flexible ecosystem.

name: Assetto Corsa Competizione
bestFor: GT3/GT4 drivers who want tire feel, brake consistency, night racing, weather, and sweaty online sessions.
hardwareThatMatters: Load-cell or better pedals first, then a rigid seat position and a wheelbase that gives front-grip detail.
watchTheTrap: A powerful base on a flexy wheel stand makes ACC feel worse, not better. Fix the brake deck before chasing torque.

name: Le Mans Ultimate
bestFor: Prototype and GT endurance fans who care about long-race comfort, brake repeatability, and display awareness.
hardwareThatMatters: Stable seating, pedals that do the same thing on lap 80, and a display setup that reduces fatigue.
watchTheTrap: Endurance exposes tiny ergonomic problems. If your neck, wrists, or brake leg complain after 30 minutes, the rig is not done.

name: Gran Turismo 7
signal: The console gateway drug with real hardware consequences.
bestFor: PS5 players who want a beautiful first serious racing setup without building a PC.
hardwareThatMatters: Console-compatible direct drive, a sturdy wheel stand or cockpit, and pedals that will not slide across the room.
watchTheTrap: Console compatibility is exact-model specific. Do not buy a PC-only base because the brand name looked familiar.

name: Forza Motorsport
bestFor: Players who want approachable racing with real wheel support and a cleaner console-first path.
hardwareThatMatters: Xbox-compatible wheelbases, a sane cockpit or stand, and pedals good enough to make braking learnable.
watchTheTrap: Xbox support often depends on the exact wheel rim or bundle, not just the base. Verify before checkout.

name: GSPro
bestFor: Garage and basement golfers who want community courses, PC graphics, and broad launch-monitor paths.
hardwareThatMatters: A compatible launch monitor, Windows gaming PC, impact screen, projector or display, and reliable networking.
watchTheTrap: Software compatibility can eliminate a launch monitor before you ever hit a ball. Pick the software path early.

name: E6 Connect
signal: The polished cross-device golf sim option.
bestFor: Golfers who want a more appliance-like software experience, especially when iPad support matters.
hardwareThatMatters: The launch monitor/software compatibility pair, then room geometry, mat, screen, and projector.
watchTheTrap: The wrong software-monitor pair can create subscription costs or missing features you did not budget for.

name: Awesome Golf
bestFor: Families, kids, practice games, and golfers who want the bay used by more than one obsessive adult.
hardwareThatMatters: A launch monitor it supports, safe side protection, a durable mat, and an enclosure that can survive weird shots.
watchTheTrap: Fun software does not make a cheap net safe. Protect walls, ceilings, and people first.

name: Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024
bestFor: GA pilots, airliner builders, bush flyers, sightseeing pilots, and anyone who wants the room to feel like a cockpit.
hardwareThatMatters: Yoke or stick, throttle quadrant, rudders, head tracking, and powered USB before panel sprawl.
watchTheTrap: Panels are fun but not first. A weak USB chain or missing rudders will annoy you more than a missing radio stack.

name: X-Plane 12
bestFor: Pilots who care about flight feel, aircraft systems, procedure practice, and a serious controls workflow.
hardwareThatMatters: Accurate primary controls, rudder pedals, trim workflow, and head tracking for cockpit scanning.
watchTheTrap: Do not build a panel farm until the physical control loop feels natural.

name: DCS World
signal: The HOTAS and button-box final boss.
bestFor: Combat aviation pilots who want deep aircraft modules, HOTAS muscle memory, and serious cockpit workflow.
hardwareThatMatters: Quality HOTAS, rudder pedals, mounts, head tracking or VR, and enough buttons to stop touching the keyboard.
watchTheTrap: DCS can make cheap controls feel cheap fast. Buy fewer pieces, but buy the ones your aircraft actually needs.

name: Star Citizen
bestFor: Pilots who want six-axis flight, dogfighting, hauling, mining, and a desk that starts looking like a cockpit.
hardwareThatMatters: Dual sticks or HOSAS, mounts, head tracking, button layers, and a binding plan you can remember under pressure.
watchTheTrap: A gorgeous dual-stick setup without mounts and bindings becomes expensive desk clutter.

name: Elite Dangerous
bestFor: Explorers, traders, combat pilots, and people who want a cockpit that feels good for hours.
hardwareThatMatters: HOTAS or HOSAS, comfortable mounts, head tracking, and enough buttons for menus without keyboard fishing.
watchTheTrap: Comfort beats maximal controls. A painful mount position ruins deep-space sessions.

name: Sailaway
signal: The quiet marine sim escape hatch.
bestFor: Sailing fans who care about weather, route planning, and a calmer sim night than another ranked race.
hardwareThatMatters: A comfortable desk, optional wheel/helm mapping where supported, and controls that do not overcomplicate the sim.
watchTheTrap: Marine sims often have uneven hardware support. Verify analog input before buying dedicated controls.

name: Bridge Command
bestFor: Builders who want a practical ship bridge training sim and are willing to DIY controls.
hardwareThatMatters: Repurposed throttles, button boxes, DIY USB boards, labels, and patience.
watchTheTrap: There is no mass-market consumer ship helm. Marine control builds are repurposing and DIY by default.

name: American Truck Simulator
bestFor: Long-haul drivers, map explorers, fleet role-players, and builders who want a relaxed driving cockpit with serious control depth.
hardwareThatMatters: A 400 mm truck wheel, reachable shifter and range/splitter controls, stalks or labeled buttons, stable pedals, and a monitor close enough to survive the deeper seating position.
watchTheTrap: A premium wheel does not replace truck controls. Price the shifter, clutch, stalks, mounts, USB, and desk depth before comparing bundles.

name: Euro Truck Simulator 2
bestFor: Drivers who want deep map coverage, long routes, manual control routines, and a simulator that turns a desk into a working cab.
hardwareThatMatters: Large wheel, range/splitter workflow, indicator and wiper controls, three pedals for manual play, and seating geometry that keeps the display close.
watchTheTrap: Do not buy 1800-degree steering before measuring the desk. A large wheel and angled base can move you far enough back to make the monitor feel smaller.

name: Farming Simulator 25
signal: The work-control simulator hiding inside a driving game.
bestFor: Players who enjoy field work, loaders, telehandlers, forestry, excavators, and building a calm working cab instead of another race cockpit.
hardwareThatMatters: A reliable 900-degree wheel, stable pedals, a proportional work stick for machinery, enough clamp clearance, and clean USB mapping.
watchTheTrap: Do not replace a good wheel to fix awkward loader controls. Add the missing work stick first, then upgrade steering only if steering is the actual problem.

name: Evercade
signal: The sim-room side station for physical-media people.
bestFor: Builders who want a tidy retro cartridge shelf beside the cockpit, golf bay, or flight desk.
hardwareThatMatters: VS-R or EXP-R hardware, HDMI or handheld plan, cartridge storage, and a budget that does not raid the main rig.
watchTheTrap: It is a lifestyle add-on, not a simulator upgrade. Buy it after the rig works.
